perturbation.original_text: Operator shall maintain statutory worker's compensation insurance, covering all of its and its Affiliates' employees and statutory employees, in accordance with the benefits afforded by the statutory Worker's Compensation Acts applicable to the state, territory, or district of hire, supervision, or place of accident. In addition, Operator shall maintain employer's liability insurance with a limit of not less than one million dollars ($1,000,000) each accident, one million dollars ($1,000,000) disease each employee, and one million dollars ($1,000,000) disease policy limit.
perturbation.changed_text: Operator shall maintain worker's compensation insurance, covering only its directly employed personnel, with benefits determined at Operator's discretion. In addition, Operator shall maintain employer's liability insurance as deemed necessary by Operator, with limits at the minimum state-required levels.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces several contradictions to standard worker's compensation requirements. It limits coverage to 'directly employed personnel', potentially excluding statutory employees required to be covered by law, and grants the Operator discretion in determining benefits, which conflicts with statutory benefit schedules. Limiting employer's liability insurance to 'minimum state-required levels' might also be insufficient for actual liability.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Louisiana Revised Statute 23:1021 defines 'employee' broadly for worker's compensation purposes, including many individuals beyond 'directly employed personnel'.  Also, the contract language grants the Operator discretion to determine benefits, which directly contradicts the statutory benefits schedules detailed in the Louisiana Worker's Compensation Law.

perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts worker's compensation laws. The original text mandates coverage for 'all of its and its Affiliates' employees and statutory employees,' in line with applicable Worker's Compensation Acts. The changed text limits coverage to 'directly employed personnel,' potentially excluding statutory employees who are legally required to be covered. Additionally, the original text requires specific employer's liability insurance limits, while the changed text reduces these limits to 'minimum state-required levels,' which may be insufficient. The Law Explanation confirms this by stating that the contract language grants the Operator discretion to determine benefits, which directly contradicts the statutory benefits schedules detailed in the Louisiana Worker's Compensation Law. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 references repealed legislation, the core issue remains the conflict with statutory worker's compensation requirements, as highlighted by the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Operator shall comply, in the performance of its duties and responsibilities hereunder, in all respects with all Environmental Laws and all Environmental Permits.
perturbation.changed_text: Operator shall endeavor to comply with Environmental Laws and Environmental Permits, but only to the extent reasonably practicable and economically feasible.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a significant loophole by making compliance with Environmental Laws conditional on 'reasonableness' and 'economic feasibility'. This directly contradicts the strict liability and mandatory compliance often required by environmental regulations.  The phrase 'endeavor to comply' weakens the obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Federal Clean Water Act imposes strict, non-discretionary obligations to comply with effluent limitations and permitting requirements. The added qualifiers of 'reasonably practicable and economically feasible' directly undermine the mandatory nature of these obligations, creating a pathway for non-compliance based on Operator's subjective assessment.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Summary of the Clean Water Act | US EPA Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Summary of the Clean Water Act Quick Links 2018 version of CWA from the U.S. Code (233 pp, 1.23 MB) The official text of the CWA continues to be available in the United States Code from the US Government Printing Office 33 U.S.C. §1251 et seq. (1972) The Clean Water Act (CWA) establishes the basic structure for regulating discharges of pollutants into the waters of the United States and regulating quality standards for surface waters. The basis of the CWA was enacted in 1948 and was called the Federal Water Pollution Control Act, but the Act was significantly reorganized and expanded in 1972. "Clean Water Act" became the Act's common name with amendments in 1972. Under the CWA, EPA has implemented pollution control programs such as setting wastewater standards for industry. EPA has also developed national water quality criteria recommendations for pollutants in surface waters. The CWA made it unlawful to discharge any pollutant from a point source into navigable waters, unless a permit was obtained: EPA's National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System (NPDES) permit program controls discharges. Point sources are discrete conveyances such as pipes or man-made ditches. Individual homes that are connected to a municipal system, use a septic system, or do not have a surface discharge do not need a NPDES permit; Industrial, municipal, and other facilities must obtain permits if their discharges go directly to surface waters. Compliance and Enforcement Clean Water Act Compliance Monitoring : investigations and inspections Water Enforcement History of this Act EPA History: Federal Water Pollution Control Act Amendments of 1972 History of the Clean Water Act Archived EPA History: Clean Water Act More Information The Office of Water (OW) ensures drinking water is safe, and restores and maintains oceans, watersheds, and their aquatic ecosystems to protect human health, support economic and recreational activities, and provide healthy habitat for fish, plants, and wildlife. The EPA Watershed Academy provides training courses on statutes, watershed protection, and other key Clean Water Act resources. Laws & Regulations Open Sidenav Menu Close Sidenav Menu Contact Us Contact Us to ask a question
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the original obligation to comply with environmental laws by adding the qualifier 'but only to the extent reasonably practicable and economically feasible.' This contradicts the Clean Water Act's mandate for strict compliance with pollution discharge regulations. The EPA summary states it is 'unlawful to discharge any pollutant from a point source into navigable waters, unless a permit was obtained.' By making compliance conditional, the changed text creates a loophole for non-compliance based on the Operator's assessment of feasibility, directly undermining the Act's strict requirements.

perturbation.original_text: Owner may terminate this Agreement at any time upon the occurrence of any of the following: (a) the dissolution or bankruptcy of Operator; (b) Operator fails to pay when due any amount due and payable hereunder, including, without limitation, any interest, fees, reimbursements or indemnifications, and such failure shall continue for a period of 15 days after the due date thereof; (c) other than as set forth in clause (b) above, Operator breaches or fails to observe or perform any material term, condition, or obligation contained in this Agreement and fails to correct, or fails to diligently pursue correction of, such breach within 60 days after receipt of written notice from Owner of any such breach; (d) Termination by Owner pursuant to Section 4.1.1; or (e) Without cause on no less than seven (7) days prior written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: Owner may terminate this Agreement at any time, for any reason, with or without notice, and Operator waives all rights to contest such termination.
perturbation.explanation: This modification provides the Owner with unfettered termination rights, essentially nullifying the original conditions for termination and any implied protections for the Operator. It eliminates the requirement for cause, notice, or opportunity to cure a breach. This could be problematic under general contract law principles of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Contract Law - Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: While Delaware law allows for broad contractual freedom, the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing imposes a limit on that freedom. This covenant prevents parties from acting arbitrarily or unreasonably to deprive the other party of the fruits of the bargain.  The modified termination clause, granting the Owner absolute and unconditional termination rights, could be interpreted as a violation of this implied covenant if exercised in bad faith or to opportunistically exploit the Operator.

General Provisions Part 3 Territorial Applicability and General Rules Â§ 1-301. Territorial applicability; partiesâ power to choose applicable law. (a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this State and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this State or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a), and except as provided in subsection (c), the Uniform Commercial Code applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. (c) If one of the following provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (1) Section 2-402; (2) Sections 2A-105 and 2A-106; (3) Section 4-102; (4) Section 4A-507; (5) Section 5-116; (6) Section 8-110; (7) Sections 9-301 through 9-307; (8) Section 12-107. 5A Del. The parties, by agreement, may determine the standards by which the performance of those obliga
perturbation.justification: The modified text gives the Owner unfettered termination rights, removing the original conditions and protections for the Operator. This directly contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which Delaware law recognizes. Scraped Law Snippet 1, particularly section (b) of Â§ 1-302, states that "The obligations of good faith, diligence, reasonableness, and care prescribed by the Uniform Commercial Code may not be disclaimed by agreement." The modified text, by granting the Owner absolute termination rights, allows for the potential of acting arbitrarily or unreasonably, undermining the Operator's expected benefits from the agreement. This constitutes a clear contradiction of the law's intent to ensure fair dealing in contractual relationships, as the Operator waives all rights to contest such termination. Therefore, the modification is not just a weakening, but a direct contradiction of good faith.
